subject: Endocrinology
question: A 20-year-old man with type 1 diabetes presents with abdominal pain, vomiting, and confusion. Glucose 450 mg/dL, pH 7.12, HCO3 8 mEq/L, ketones positive. What is the most important initial treatment?

options.text: IV Normal Saline bolus followed by continuous infusion
options.explanation: Volume resuscitation is the FIRST and most critical step in DKA management. Start with 1-2L IV NS in first hour, then 250-500mL/hr. Fluid deficit typically 5-10L. Insulin is started AFTER initial fluids to prevent hypokalemia and cerebral edema.

options.text: IV insulin bolus 0.1 units/kg followed by infusion
options.explanation: Insulin is essential but should be started AFTER initial fluid resuscitation and confirmation of potassium >3.3. Starting insulin first risks worsening hypokalemia and cardiac arrhythmias.
Bicarbonate is NOT routinely recommended in DKA (pH 7.12). Reserved for pH <6.9. Risks include worsening intracellular acidosis, hypokalemia, and cerebral edema. Fluids and insulin correct acidosis.
Potassium replacement is critical but after checking serum K+. Total body K+ is depleted but serum K+ may be normal or high initially due to acidosis. Replace K+ when <5.2 and patient voiding.
SC insulin is NOT appropriate for DKA management. IV regular insulin infusion is standard (0.1 units/kg/hr). SC insulin has unpredictable absorption in volume-depleted patients.

educationalObjective: Manage DKA: 1) IV fluids first, 2) Check potassium, 3) IV insulin infusion, 4) Monitor glucose and electrolytes hourly. Identify and treat precipitating cause.
keyTakeaway: DKA: Fluids FIRST (NS 1-2L bolus). Then insulin (IV infusion). Replace K+ if <5.2. Bicarb only if pH <6.9. Monitor glucose q1h.
